title: Chronic fatigue with a known inflammatory condition

chart.oneLiner: 68M with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis presents with several months of fatigue and mild breathlessness; routine labs showed anemia.

opening: I've just been dragging for months, and my doctor said my blood count is low.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man with chronic joint disease, fatigued. Good historian.

HPI: Several months of fatigue and mild exertional breathlessness. Known rheumatoid arthritis with ongoing joint pain and stiffness (active inflammation). NO black/bloody stools, NO obvious bleeding, NO weight loss, NO heavy alcohol. On methotrexate (only if asked about medications).
PMH: rheumatoid arthritis, hypertension. MEDS: methotrexate, folic acid, lisinopril, occasional acetaminophen. SOCIAL: minimal alcohol, nonsmoker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: mild pallor; chronic joint deformities of the hands if examined.
- Vitals: BP 130/78, HR 82, RR 14, 36.9°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Cardiac: regular, no murmur. Abdomen: soft, nontender. Rectal: brown stool, no melena (if examined).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the rheumatoid arthritis activity or methotrexate unless asked about medical history or medications.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: Hgb 10.2, MCV 84 (normocytic, sometimes mildly low), RDW normal, WBC/Plt normal.
labs.IronStudies: Serum iron LOW, TIBC LOW (key distinction from iron deficiency), ferritin NORMAL-to-HIGH (acute-phase reactant), transferrin saturation low-normal.
labs.BMP: Cr 1.1 — normal renal function (so not primarily CKD anemia).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: QRS >120 ms, broad monophasic R in I/V6, deep QS in V1; discordant ST-T.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: QRS >120 ms, broad monophasic R in I/V6, deep QS in V1; discordant ST-T. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Bilateral basal reticular opacities with reduced lung volumes (interstitial fibrosis).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Bilateral basal reticular opacities with reduced lung volumes (interstitial fibrosis). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing pulmonary fibrosis reticulation

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this anemia (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Anemia of chronic disease / inflammation (leading,7). Iron deficiency anemia (must distinguish,5). Anemia of chronic kidney disease (core,3). Myelodysplastic syndrome (CANT-MISS to consider,3). Medication/marrow suppression e.g. methotrexate (core,3). Names anemia of chronic disease and the plan to distinguish it from iron deficiency (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Anemia of chronic disease / inflammation. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Myelodysplastic syndrome. Also reasonable: Iron deficiency anemia; Anemia of chronic kidney disease; methotrexate.

coreActions.item: Names anemia of chronic disease and the plan to distinguish it from iron deficiency

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC with indices (CANT-MISS,3). Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC to distinguish from iron deficiency (CANT-MISS,5). Reticulocyte count (2). Inflammatory markers ESR/CRP (2). Renal function (2). B12/folate (1). PENALTY: reflexively starting iron without confirming a true deficiency (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: CBC with indices; Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC to distinguish from iron deficiency.

criticalActions.item: Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC to distinguish from iron deficiency

penalties.item: reflexively starting iron without confirming a true deficiency

requiredTests: Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC to distinguish from iron deficiency
contraindicatedTests: reflexively starting iron without confirming a true deficiency
orderSensitiveActions: reflexively starting iron without confirming a true deficiency

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal sinus rhythm, no acute changes. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes a normal tracing (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-reading (-2).
keyFindings: wide QRS >120 ms with broad notched R in lateral leads
commonMistakes: over-reading the discordant ST changes as ischemia
missing the broad monophasic lateral R wave
steps.idealAnswer: Left bundle branch block: QRS >120 ms, broad notched R in I/V6, dominant S in V1, appropriately discordant ST-T.

coreActions: wide QRS >120 ms with broad notched R in lateral leads

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes it can screen for an inflammatory/infectious driver (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Pulmonary fibrosis: bibasilar reticular opacities with low lung volumes.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Normocytic anemia with low iron, LOW TIBC, and normal/high ferritin plus elevated inflammatory markers. What is your diagnosis and how does it differ from iron deficiency?
steps.rubric: Anemia of chronic disease (6). Distinguishes from iron deficiency — in chronic disease TIBC is LOW and ferritin normal/high, whereas iron deficiency shows HIGH TIBC and LOW ferritin (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH anemia of chronic disease: treat the underlying disease (CANT-MISS,7); confirm iron studies distinguishing ACD from IDA (5); transfuse only if symptomatic/unstable (4); iron only if concurrent true deficiency (3); ESA only in select settings with specialist input (2). PENALTY: transfusing/ironing a stable ACD patient reflexively (-2); missing an underlying malignancy/infection (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Treat the underlying chronic inflammatory/infectious/malignant disease – that is the primary therapy. Confirm with iron studies (low iron, normal/high ferritin). Avoid unnecessary transfusion; transfuse only if symptomatic. Replace iron only if coexisting true iron deficiency. Consider ESAs only in specific settings (e.g. CKD) with hematology input.

bonusActions.item: Consider ESA in select settings (e.g. CKD)

optionalActions: Consider ESA in select settings (e.g. CKD)

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis presents with several months of fatigue and mild breathlessness; routine labs showed anemia. The diagnosis is Anemia of chronic disease, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular, no murmur. Abdomen: soft, nontender. Rectal: brown stool, no melena (if examined).
General: mild pallor; chronic joint deformities of the hands if examined.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Anemia of chronic disease while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Anemia of chronic disease and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalWorkupItems: Iron studies — ferritin/iron/TIBC to distinguish from iron deficiency
criticalInterpretationItems: Normal sinus rhythm; no acute changes.
No acute process (may screen for an inflammatory/infectious driver).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
